questions.questionText: What is Newton's First Law of Motion also known as?
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law is also called the Law of Inertia because it describes how objects resist changes in their state of motion.
hints: Think about what makes an object resist motion changes
It's related to an object's tendency to maintain its current state

questions.questionText: According to Newton's First Law, an object at rest will stay at rest unless acted upon by what?
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law states that an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion, unless acted upon by an external force.
hints: What causes changes in motion?
It must come from outside the object

questions.questionText: A book is resting on a table. Why doesn't it move?
options: There are no forces acting on it
The forces acting on it are balanced
Gravity is not acting on it
questions.explanation: The book experiences gravity pulling it down and the normal force from the table pushing it up. These forces are balanced, so the book remains at rest.
hints: Forces can be present but still result in no movement
Think about equal and opposite forces

questions.questionText: What property of matter causes objects to resist changes in their motion?
questions.explanation: Inertia is directly related to an object's mass. Objects with more mass have more inertia and resist changes in motion more strongly.
hints: It's a measure of the amount of matter
Heavier objects are harder to push or stop

questions.questionText: A car is moving at constant velocity on a straight road. What is the net force acting on the car?
options: Opposite to the direction of motion
questions.explanation: When an object moves at constant velocity (no acceleration), the net force on it is zero. All forces are balanced.
hints: Constant velocity means no change in motion
What does Newton's First Law say about objects in uniform motion?

questions.questionText: Which of the following best demonstrates inertia?
options: A ball rolling down a hill
Your body jerks forward when a car suddenly stops
questions.explanation: When a car stops suddenly, your body continues moving forward due to inertia - the tendency to maintain its state of motion.
hints: Look for an example where something resists a change in motion
What happens when motion suddenly changes?

questions.questionText: If you're in a spacecraft in deep space (far from any gravitational influence) and you throw a ball, what will happen to the ball?
options: It will fall to the floor of the spacecraft
It will continue moving in a straight line at constant velocity
It will curve back to you
It will slow down and stop
questions.explanation: In the absence of external forces (like friction or gravity), the ball will continue moving at constant velocity in a straight line, as stated by Newton's First Law.
hints: Consider what forces are acting on the ball
Remember Newton's First Law applies when no external forces act

questions.questionText: A 2kg object and a 5kg object are both at rest. Which statement is true?
questions.explanation: Inertia is directly proportional to mass. The 5kg object has more mass, therefore more inertia, meaning it resists changes in motion more than the 2kg object.
hints: Inertia depends on mass
Which object has more matter?

questions.questionText: A hockey puck sliding on ice eventually comes to a stop. Which statement best explains why this doesn't violate Newton's First Law?
options: Newton's First Law only applies to objects in vacuum
Friction acts as an external force to slow the puck
The puck loses mass as it slides
Newton's First Law doesn't apply to sports equipment
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law states objects continue in motion unless acted upon by an external force. Friction between the puck and ice is an external force that opposes the motion, causing it to slow and stop.
hints: What force opposes motion on surfaces?
Is there an external force acting on the puck?

questions.questionText: Two astronauts are floating in space, initially at rest relative to each other. Astronaut A (60kg) pushes Astronaut B (90kg). Immediately after the push, which astronaut will have greater speed?
options: Both will have the same speed
questions.explanation: When Astronaut A pushes B, Newton's Third Law applies (equal and opposite forces). However, Astronaut A has less mass, so experiences greater acceleration (F=ma). With less inertia, Astronaut A achieves higher speed than Astronaut B.
hints: Consider both Newton's First and Third Laws
Which astronaut has less inertia?
Remember F = ma
